title: Add pasta networking mode
body:
### Description
This introduces a new user-mode networking option using [`pasta(1)`](https://passt.top/passt/about/#pasta-pack-a-subtle-tap-abstraction).

_pasta_ implements a translation layer between a Layer-2 network interface (a tap device inside a network namespace) and native Layer-4 sockets (TCP, UDP, ICMP/ICMPv6 echo) on a host. It doesn't require any capabilities or privileges, and it can be used as a **simple, purpose-written, high performance replacement** for [`slirp4netns(1)`](https://github.com/rootless-containers/slirp4netns).

Similarly to slirp4netns, there's no need to create further interfaces on the host. This approach doesn't require any capabilities or privileges.

_pasta_ also implements a _tap bypass path_ for local connections **only**: packets with a local destination address can be moved directly between Layer-4 sockets, avoiding Layer-2 translations, using the `splice(2)` and `recvmmsg(2)`/`sendmmsg(2)` system calls for TCP and UDP, respectively.

A few notable differences compared to `slirp4netns(1)`:

* _pasta_ forks to background once networking is configured in the namespace and quits on its own once the namespace is deleted: file descriptor synchronisation and PID tracking are not needed

* port forwarding is configured via command line options at start-up, instead of an API socket: this is taken care of right away as we're about to start _pasta_

* there's no need for further selection of port forwarding modes: pasta behaves similarly to [containers-rootlessport](https://github.com/rootless-containers/rootlesskit/tree/master/pkg/port) for local binds (`splice()` instead of `read()`/`write()` pairs, without L2-L4 translation), and keeps the original source address for non-local connections like slirp4netns does
    
* IPv6 is not an experimental feature. **IPv6 port forwarding is supported**
    
* by default, addresses and routes are copied from the host, that is, container users will see the same IP address and routes as if they were in the _init_ namespace context. The interface name is also sourced from the host upstream interface with the first default route in the routing table. This is also configurable as documented
    
* by default, the host is reachable using the gateway address from the container, unless the `--no-map-gw` option is passed

* **sandboxing** and **`seccomp(2)`** policies cannot be disabled

See also this [demo](https://passt.top/passt/about/#pasta_2) showing pasta operation with Podman. Throughput and latency [figures](https://passt.top#perf_27) for TCP and UDP, over IPv4 and IPv6, are regularly obtained by CI runs.

### Availability
I'm submitting this now after originally [proposing](https://lists.podman.io/archives/list/podman@lists.podman.io/thread/PHIFAJLUNI3UKNIIF5HFIQNTNGQ5PUZE/) this a few months ago given that packages for some distributions ([Fedora](https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/passt), [openSUSE](https://build.opensuse.org/package/show/home:dfaggioli:devel/passt)) are now available.

I maintain **unofficial** packages for other RPM-based distributions (CentOS Stream, EPEL, Mageia) on my Fedora Copr [repository](https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/sbrivio/passt/).

**Unofficial** x86_64 [packages](https://passt.top/builds/latest/x86_64/) for Debian and Debian-based distributions are currently derived from static builds, and there's an open [RFP](https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=1010498) for Debian.

Static build [packages](https://passt.top/builds/latest/x86_64/) are also available for other RPM-based distributions.

### Tests
This pull request introduces a number of tests, **some of which will fail** with the current upstream version of pasta, because I just found a few issues while writing those. Patches are on their [way](https://archives.passt.top/), and they should be available in packages within a few days, but I thought I would submit this early for review anyway.

### Additional notes
This also introduces support for https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14425, not via native Podman options at the moment, as commented on that issue itself: binding addresses to specific interfaces can only be done by passing _opaque options_ to pasta, as documented.

It should also address https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13229 and related issues.

@Luap99 @giuseppe @dgibson @vivier @dfaggioli @eriksjolund @cpach @baude @PhrozenByte

### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
This pull request introduces a new networking option, that can be enabled with the `--net=pasta` option. Detailed usage is described in the documentation.
```

comments.body: @Luap99 @mheon @baude @AkihiroSuda @giuseppe PTAL

thanks for working on this, from a quick look, it seems good to me.

Is there any plan to add the packages to Fedora?

@giuseppe

> thanks for working on this, from a quick look, it seems good to me.

Thanks for having a look!

> Is there any plan to add the packages to Fedora?

They're already available, see: https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/passt and the Availability section [above](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16141#issue-1406598530) for details about other distributions.
Do you have benchmark data? (container->external via TAP, and external->container via the port forwarder)
/approve

@Luap99 I don't think we can avoid a breaking change here (networks named pasta will no longer function with `podman run` and `podman create`, as they'll be redirected to the Pasta driver). However, given that `podman network connect` ought to still let folks connect to those networks, this seems OK. Do you concur, or should we investigate compatibility more thoroughly?

@flouthoc 

> It would have been really nice if we could keep the new implementation at `c/common/libnetwork` as of now slirp4netns is also duplicated in podman and buildah. Since pasta is entierly new implementation , Could we consider it implementing in `c/common` and not only in podman ?

I don't know enough about that to have an informed opinion, and the [description](https://github.com/containers/common/#containerscommon) of [common](https://github.com/containers/common/) isn't helping me much, but I have some questions:
* what would be the advantage of that?
* in which repository should the tests reside, in that case?
* do other users of [common](https://github.com/containers/common/) use the same options and structure to describe port forwarding? Because that's **55** out of the 81 lines of code in `networking_pasta.go`.

@sbrivio-rh Yes buildah is also supposed to consume this for its networking. But I think this would need a better refactoring and even introduce a top level interface, maybe some thing like`RootlessTransport` and both `slirp4netns` and `pasta` can just implement the interface `RootlessTransport`. So both `podman` and `buildah` can use this interface and requested implementations. 

But this sounds like a bigger refactor therefore I think following PR is fine and maybe can be refactored later into `containers/common` after discussing the design.

> @sbrivio-rh Yes buildah is also supposed to consume this for its networking. But I think this would need a better refactoring and even introduce a top level interface, maybe some thing like`RootlessTransport` and both `slirp4netns` and `pasta` can just implement the interface `RootlessTransport`. So both `podman` and `buildah` can use this interface and requested implementations.
> 
> But this sounds like a bigger refactor therefore I think following PR is fine and maybe can be refactored later into `containers/common` after discussing the design.

In general I agree with that assessment but I do not think it is fair to let @sbrivio-rh make to hard work of rewriting/restructuring this. The code here is simple enough, if we like to abstract this into c/common someone else can easily copy this without much issues.

@Luap99 
> You need to run make vendor and make validate,

Oh, sorry, I missed that line from `CONTRIBUTING.md`. Well,

```
$ go version
go version go1.19.2 linux/amd64
$ make vendor
go mod tidy
go mod vendor
go mod verify
all modules verified
$ make validate
VERSION=1.46.2 ./hack/install_golangci.sh
Installing golangci-lint v1.46.2 into ./bin/golangci-lint
golangci/golangci-lint info checking GitHub for tag 'v1.46.2'
golangci/golangci-lint info found version: 1.46.2 for v1.46.2/linux/amd64
golangci/golangci-lint info installed ./bin/golangci-lint
hack/golangci-lint.sh run

Running golangci-lint for tunnel
Build Tags tunnel: apparmor,seccomp,selinux,linter,remote
Skipped directories tunnel: pkg/api
panic: load embedded ruleguard rules: rules/rules.go:13: can't load fmt

goroutine 1 [running]:
github.com/go-critic/go-critic/checkers.init.22()
	github.com/go-critic/go-critic@v0.6.3/checkers/embedded_rules.go:47 +0x4b4
make: *** [Makefile:247: golangci-lint] Error 2
```
Installing the `latest` version of golanci didn't help:

```
$ git diff hack/install_golangci.sh
diff --git a/hack/install_golangci.sh b/hack/install_golangci.sh
index 29d925666..92d82658a 100755
--- a/hack/install_golangci.sh
+++ b/hack/install_golangci.sh
@@ -3,10 +3,11 @@
 die() { echo "${1:-No error message given} (from $(basename $0))"; exit 1; }
 
 [ -n "$VERSION" ] || die "\$VERSION is empty or undefined"
+case ${VERSION} in v*|latest) ;; *) VERSION="v${VERSION}" ;; esac
 
 function install() {
-    echo "Installing golangci-lint v$VERSION into $BIN"
-    curl -sSL --retry 5 https://raw.githubusercontent.com/golangci/golangci-lint/master/install.sh | sh -s v$VERSION
+    echo "Installing golangci-lint $VERSION into $BIN"
+    curl -sSL --retry 5 https://raw.githubusercontent.com/golangci/golangci-lint/master/install.sh | sh -s $VERSION
 }
 
 # Undocumented behavior: golangci-lint installer requires $BINDIR in env,
$ VERSION="latest" ./hack/install_golangci.sh
Installing golangci-lint latest into ./bin/golangci-lint
golangci/golangci-lint info checking GitHub for tag 'latest'
golangci/golangci-lint info found version: 1.50.0 for v1.50.0/linux/amd64
golangci/golangci-lint info installed ./bin/golangci-lint
```
Because `make validate` overrides that with 1.46.2 anyway:
```
$ make validate
VERSION=1.46.2 ./hack/install_golangci.sh
panic: load embedded ruleguard rules: rules/rules.go:13: can't load fmt

goroutine 1 [running]:
github.com/go-critic/go-critic/checkers.init.22()
	github.com/go-critic/go-critic@v0.6.3/checkers/embedded_rules.go:47 +0x4b4
Installing golangci-lint v1.46.2 into ./bin/golangci-lint
golangci/golangci-lint info checking GitHub for tag 'v1.46.2'
golangci/golangci-lint info found version: 1.46.2 for v1.46.2/linux/amd64
golangci/golangci-lint info installed ./bin/golangci-lint
hack/golangci-lint.sh run

Running golangci-lint for tunnel
Build Tags tunnel: apparmor,seccomp,selinux,linter,remote
Skipped directories tunnel: pkg/api
panic: load embedded ruleguard rules: rules/rules.go:13: can't load fmt

goroutine 1 [running]:
github.com/go-critic/go-critic/checkers.init.22()
	github.com/go-critic/go-critic@v0.6.3/checkers/embedded_rules.go:47 +0x4b4
make: *** [Makefile:247: golangci-lint] Error 2
```
Anyway, worked around that with:
```
$ VERSION="latest" ./hack/install_golangci.sh
Installing golangci-lint latest into ./bin/golangci-lint
golangci/golangci-lint info checking GitHub for tag 'latest'
golangci/golangci-lint info found version: 1.50.0 for v1.50.0/linux/amd64
golangci/golangci-lint info installed ./bin/golangci-lint
```
and then running directly:
```
$ ./hack/golangci-lint.sh run
```
> I suspect many code style issues.

Hmm, yes:
```
$ ./hack/golangci-lint.sh run 2>/dev/null | grep "^[a-z].*"|wc -l
121
```
but I don't seem to have added any:
```
$ ./hack/golangci-lint.sh run 2>/dev/null | grep pasta
$
```
Is there something else I'm missing?

Do you use go 1.19? see https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16011 in that case.
If you cannot get it to work locally just fix the errors reported in CI and push again until all issues are resolved.

If you remove "github.com/pkg/errors" you may not need to run make vendor, only when some deps are changed it is required.

@Luap99 

> Do you use go 1.19?

Yes, 1.19.2, see the output above.

> see #16011 in that case. If you cannot get it to work locally just fix the errors reported in CI and push again until all issues are resolved.

I already installed golangci-lint 1.50.0 with the steps I outlined above, and I only see pre-existing issues, except that:

> If you remove "github.com/pkg/errors" you may not need to run make vendor, only when some deps are changed it is required.

...I haven't addressed this comment of yours yet, I'll do that soon.

@edsantiago 

> This is way more than I can review today, sorry. Just a few comments to get started. And one more: this adds almost 200 lines to the already-bloated `helpers.bash`. Please split that into a new `helpers.network.bash` (or some other descriptive name) (not "pasta", because some of these helpers seem like they could be useful in other tests).

Done, I also restructured things a bit, mostly in terms of style.

> Oh, also, I really think some of the tests could be refactored. At a very minimum, in your new 505 file, please add a `setup` with `skip_if_no_pasta` instead of repeating that over and over in each test. (Please follow the guidelines for `setup` in the README).

Done. Actually, I parameterised all the forwarding and transfer tests based on keywords in their names, to me it looks much easier to maintain, and it's about 600 lines going away.

> * by default, the host is reachable using the gateway address from the container, unless the `--no-map-gw` option is passed
> *

Could we make this the default in Podman and have a way in pasta to override it (e.g. `--map-gw` overrides any previously configured `--no-map-gw`)?

@giuseppe 

> > * by default, the host is reachable using the gateway address from the container, unless the `--no-map-gw` option is passed
> 
> Could we make this the default in Podman and have a way in pasta to override it (e.g. `--map-gw` overrides any previously configured `--no-map-gw`)?

Ah, yes, I actually wanted to ask what the default should be. I would rather do this to avoid duplicating options: `setupPasta()` will pass `--no-map-gw`. If the user specifies `--map-gw` in pasta options, `setupPasta()` will drop the `--no-map-gw` option. For pasta as stand-alone tool it's quite practical to have a mapping to the host by default, and for Podman that would be equivalent.

I'll change this in a bit.

> @Luap99 I don't think we can avoid a breaking change here (networks named pasta will no longer function with podman run and podman create, as they'll be redirected to the Pasta driver). However, given that podman network connect ought to still let folks connect to those networks, this seems OK. Do you concur, or should we investigate compatibility more thoroughly?

I think you are right this is a breaking change. We should think about keeping support for networks with the name pasta. I think the only good option is to check if the `pasta` network exists and if it does use it over the actual pasta binary.

> > @Luap99 I don't think we can avoid a breaking change here (networks named pasta will no longer function with podman run and podman create, as they'll be redirected to the Pasta driver). However, given that podman network connect ought to still let folks connect to those networks, this seems OK. Do you concur, or should we investigate compatibility more thoroughly?
> 
> I think you are right this is a breaking change. We should think about keeping support for networks with the name pasta. I think the only good option is to check if the `pasta` network exists and if it does use it over the actual pasta binary.

I spent a couple of hours of this but I don't see a way to pass down to `ParseNetworkFlag()` a list of existing networks (which, I suppose, should come from `registry.ContainerEngine()`) that doesn't look a complete layering violation. Any pointer? Or should it be done at an upper level, after parsing?

> > > @Luap99 I don't think we can avoid a breaking change here (networks named pasta will no longer function with podman run and podman create, as they'll be redirected to the Pasta driver). However, given that podman network connect ought to still let folks connect to those networks, this seems OK. Do you concur, or should we investigate compatibility more thoroughly?
> > 
> > 
> > I think you are right this is a breaking change. We should think about keeping support for networks with the name pasta. I think the only good option is to check if the `pasta` network exists and if it does use it over the actual pasta binary.
> 
> I spent a couple of hours of this but I don't see a way to pass down to `ParseNetworkFlag()` a list of existing networks (which, I suppose, should come from `registry.ContainerEngine()`) that doesn't look a complete layering violation. Any pointer? Or should it be done at an upper level, after parsing?


Oh yeah this is going to look ugly, maybe add a bool pastaNetworkExist to `ParseNetworkFlag()` and then to a `NetworkInspect("pasta")` before and set the bool accordingly. Definitely something I would like to remove when we bump the next major version.

> Oh yeah this is going to look ugly, maybe add a bool pastaNetworkExist to `ParseNetworkFlag()` and then to a `NetworkInspect("pasta")` before and set the bool accordingly. Definitely something I would like to remove when we bump the next major version.

In the end, I found out I could use `registry.ContainerEngine().NetworkList(registry.Context(), networkListOptions)` from cmd/podman/pods/create.go and cmd/podman/containers/create.go. Not much prettier than the alternative, but getting from a place with a `runtime` object down to there looked even more involved.

Yeah... I've been reviewing it off and on all day. It's just way too huge, and way too hyperactive, and I don't touch things that are hyperactive. I let things rest. I've been trying to break down the changes in a way that make it possible for me to review. If this has settled down by morning, it will be my first priority. Thanks again for your hard work on this.

>can you please indicate in which way tests are broken?

"Tests passing" != "Tests are working". None of these tests run in CI, obviously. And see my comment just now about `[0-f]`.

BTW, my goal in providing review feedback is to ensure that, when this merges, it is maintainable and robust. This is impressive work, very promising, and I'm thankful that you've taken the time to write a comprehensive set of tests.
I'm currently travelling to a conference. I intend to follow up with the latest set of requests, but there might be delays.

> The multiple commits are really hard to read. [02fe644](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/02fe644be3e0e26637d6184da92fcf180550c9f4) should be combined into [71a025c](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/71a025c4ad884f32985d58369f5d0e0d79218d7f), it's purely a private-to-you difference that need not be seen by any future maintainer.

Not really, those are two logically distinct changes: in the latter I'm moving functions, in the former I'm reformatting them. I could have done each one independently and consistently, hence the value of submitting them as separate changes. Those are really basic contribution rules of most FLOSS projects I've ever contributed to since `cvs` was around. If Podman is so different in this regard, I think it would be valuable to have a different wording in `CONTRIBUTING.md`, because yes, it says:

> When in doubt, squash.

while at the same time keeping the basic, sensible idea:

> Squash your commits into logical pieces of work that might want to be reviewed separate from the rest of the PRs

(where I suppose "PRs" was meant to be "commits").

> And the correct polite way to do the file split is to spawn off `helpers.network.bash` **before** [68ed10a](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/68ed10a3f550801c6f6bf6d51def9274e5e3462c), then add your pasta helpers in the commit where you add the tests. Adding pasta stuff to `helpers.bash`, then immediately moving it out, is really confusing to a reader. (To put it another way: it reflects your personal way of having done it; a git history should instead reflect a logical progression of changes).

I actually did it the other way, and rebased it around, because, more logically, I'm first adding stuff related to pasta, and then other things, not strictly related, that you requested. I don't think it belongs in the same pull request, as it wouldn't belong in the same patchset, but if it really needs to be there, good, let me at least put that at the end, please.

> I'm not going to block, because I've crabbed too much already. Others on the team have strong opinions, too, about git commit history - I will let them approve or request changes as they wish. And, I have other non-git-history-related concerns, but I will clean those up myself in a future PR. The important thing is, thank you for the code and the tests.

You're welcome.

I feel very strongly that your commits are improperly sequenced. The `helpers.net` split looks like an afterthought (because it is). I'm more fond of the Plan-Ahead model.

Fortunately for all of us I don't work Fridays or weekends, so this review is now in the hands of other Containers team members. Thanks again for contributing, I genuinely believe that this is promising new functionality, and am grateful for your efforts.

You're welcome! Thanks for your reviews.

To be honest, I still I see it this way, rephrasing: there was no issue with helpers.bash. I introduced the issue by adding more lines (I honestly didn't see any but you may be right), and then I fixed it. If I kept it the original way I had in my tree (first move, then add) I would be fixing a non-issue. Well, let's see if anybody has further opinions on that. At the same time, just moving lines in a commit, instead of moving and formatting, makes things a bit easier `git-blame`-wise (in case of breakage: 1. Did they really just move things here? Okay, yes. 2. Check next commit)

Hi again. I still feel rather strongly that your commit sequencing can be improved, so please bear with me as I try to explain myself again:
```
* fffffffff (HEAD -> mypr)   [300+ lines]
| oops, that wasn't quite right, reorder and reindent
| Reviewer still wasn't happy with my last commit,
| so let's try this
|
* eeeeeeeee     [almost 800 lines]
| Move new code to a separate module
| Dang, that previous commit added a lot to an already-bloated
| system file. Let's move all the new code to an auxiliary module.
| And, while we're at it, I found more functions that could be
| moved to the new module.
|
* ddddddddd    [over 800 lines]
| My new fluffybunnies code!
```
Compare to:
```
* ccccccccc  (HEAD -> mypr)
| My new fluffybunnies code!
| New fluffybunny module, with a few additions to the 
| auxiliary all-bunnies module.
|
* bbbbbbbbb
| Refactor: move bunny-related code to auxiliary module
| In preparation for my new fluffybunnies PR, move existing
| scratchy-bunny code to an auxiliary all-bunnies module.
| 
| This is just a cut/paste from one large module to a new one, plus
| includes where needed.
```
My argument is: the latter shows consideration for the reviewer. Small, tight diffs. The refactor is smaller, obviously, because only existing code is moved. It looks like planning, not a series of afterthoughts. The "new fluffybunnies" commit is probably identical in both cases (modulo one filename). Which one would you prefer to review?

> Hi again. I still feel rather strongly that your commit sequencing can be improved, so please bear with me as I try to explain myself again:
> 
> ```
> * fffffffff (HEAD -> mypr)   [300+ lines]
> | oops, that wasn't quite right, reorder and reindent
> | Reviewer still wasn't happy with my last commit,
> | so let's try this
> ```

Note that this is not what the current `fffffffff` is about. I'm reordering and reformatting _existing_ code, and yes, making it consistent with the one I just introduced, but that's unavoidable.

> ```
> * eeeeeeeee     [almost 800 lines]
> | Move new code to a separate module
> | Dang, that previous commit added a lot to an already-bloated
> | system file. Let's move all the new code to an auxiliary module.
> ```

This is accurate, but:

> ```
> | And, while we're at it, I found more functions that could be
> | moved to the new module.
> ```

I don't understand this. I'm just moving *all* the networking functions, that's the only point of that commit, and that's why it makes sense to have it as a commit.

> ```
> * ddddddddd    [over 800 lines]
> | My new fluffybunnies code!
> ```
> 
> Compare to:
> 
> ```
> * ccccccccc  (HEAD -> mypr)
> | My new fluffybunnies code!
> | New fluffybunny module, with a few additions to the 
> | auxiliary all-bunnies module.
> |
> * bbbbbbbbb
> | Refactor: move bunny-related code to auxiliary module
> | In preparation for my new fluffybunnies PR, move existing
> | scratchy-bunny code to an auxiliary all-bunnies module.
> | 
> | This is just a cut/paste from one large module to a new one, plus
> | includes where needed.
> ```

The second version is missing reformatting and reordering (that is, `fffffffff`).

> My argument is: the latter shows consideration for the reviewer. Small, tight diffs. The refactor is smaller, obviously, because only existing code is moved. It looks like planning, not a series of afterthoughts. The "new fluffybunnies" commit is probably identical in both cases (modulo one filename). Which one would you prefer to review?

I would have a *slight* preference for `ddddddddd`, `eeeeeeeee`, `fffffffff`, because it includes `fffffffff`, and other than that, reviewing a big code move should be the same as reviewing a slightly smaller code move. Anyway, given the existing situation where things can only be found by `grep`, better than nothing.

I still think it's a bad idea to have `fffffffff` and `eeeeeeeee` (or `bbbbbbbbb`) in the same pull request as `ddddddddd` (or `ccccccccc`), as that roughly seems to correspond to a patchset, and the resulting clean-up is really not functional to `ddddddddd` (or `ccccccccc`).

In any case, you're blocking this, so I'll go with the `bbbbbbbbb`, `ccccccccc`, `fffffffff` version in a moment. If you want me to leave behind inconsistent formatting across both 1. existing code 2. existing and new code, I can just drop `fffffffff`, it would still be at the end.

No, skip it. You have your argument, and you're the proponent for this, and I'm not going to review this again... so leave it as is.

@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL at the networking code itself. Tests will have to be OK as they are.

> No, skip it. You have your argument, and you're the proponent for this, and I'm not going to review this again... so leave it as is.

Okay, I had just started, but I'm more than happy to stop, and it makes sense to me, thanks for understanding.
@flouthoc is there any reason why you're blocking this? Anything I can address?
I forgot to update this: @Luap99 shared a number of test failures happening in his environment, offline. I'm working on those now.

The latest version of the commits:
* reorders them as requested by @edsantiago: one commit moves and reformats helpers, the next commit adds new tests and helpers
* prevents `pasta` from being started as root in root mode, skips tests unless rootless mode is selected, and documents that pasta networking can only be used for rootless mode, in man page and commit message
* fixes other issues reported by @Luap99 in tests, namely missing `skip` directives when IPv6 is not available, wrong handling of pasta options when multiple interfaces with a default route are available, client delay too short for large UDP transfer.

Those should run properly with upstream version `20221026.ge4df8b0`, also built as `0^20221026.ge4df8b0-1` for RPM-based distributions at https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/sbrivio/passt/build/4991606/ and permanently mirrored at https://passt.top/builds/copr/0^20221026.ge4df8b0/, and available as static build binaries and Debian-like packages (x86_64 only) at https://passt.top/builds/latest/x86_64/.

A fix for a further issue reported by @Luap99 (first ping reply not received with Busybox's ping) is pending upstream review: https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20221026162531.545374-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/ -- this shouldn't overly impact Podman tests, though.

>    #/vvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvv
>    #| FAIL: exit code is 1; expected 0

This comes from the fact that (I guess) you have `systemd-resolved` running on your host, and a loopback (probably `127.0.0.53`) address configured as DNS. In that case, pasta refuses to use that address with Podman (because of `--no-map-gw`) as it knows that the container won't be able to reach that address. However, we should still use that as target for the forwarder, but not advertise it to the container itself.

This should be fixed by https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20221102230443.377446-3-sbrivio@redhat.com/, now pending review.

These two issues are due to the fact that, if the maximum size of socket sending buffers is smaller than what  `socat` tries to send (`/proc/sys/net/core/wmem_default` is 256 KiB by default on Fedora 36), `socat` will just fill them up, fail to send most of the test file, and quit.

I changed the size for UDP transfers in the new version of the commits, just pushed. It's now `wmem_default` divided by 4: the kernel conventionally accounts for buffer bookkeeping by doubling the value if set via socket option (but not via file), and we need to have additional margin for our datagrams, hence this factor.

> ```
>  ✗ podman networking with pasta(1) - IPv4 default route
>    (from function `assert' in file test/system/helpers.bash, line 626,

>    $ /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/bin/podman images --all --format {{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}} {{.ID}}

>    []
>    #/vvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvv
>    #|     FAIL: Container route not matching host
>    #| expected: = '10.156.0.1'
>    #|   actual:   'null'
>    #\^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
> ```

This, and a number of subsequent, related failures, is due to a rather particular configuration of routing on the host, which pasta inherits and fails to configure in the container. The address is configured with a /32 netmask, so it shouldn't be able to have any associated route, but this is made possible by a /32 route just for the gateway. This is configured via `cloud-init-dhcp`, and this information comes straight from the DHCP server -- I have no idea why.

This should be worked around by a consistency check added in https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20221102230443.377446-2-sbrivio@redhat.com/: if the netmask doesn't allow reaching the gateway, shrink it until it does.

This is due to the same issue you hit in your setup. Once that's fixed, for some reason 169.254.169.254 answers our query for 127.0.0.1 correctly reporting `1.0.0.127.in-addr.arpa` as name, and at the same time reporting it as `NXDOMAIN`, which is absurd.

I adjusted the tests to take this into account: resolution might report `NXDOMAIN`, but what matters is that we actually reach a server telling us the `1.0.0.127.in-addr.arpa` name.

>    2022/11/01 07:44:22 socat[12660] E connect(8, AF=2 10.156.0.16:5635, 16): Interrupted system call
>    timeout: sending signal TERM to command ‘/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/bin/podman’
>    timeout: sending signal KILL to command ‘/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/bin/podman’
>    [ rc=124 (** EXPECTED 0 **) ]
>    *** TIMED OUT ***
> ```

All these are due to the missing IPv4 default route.

Other changes from the latest revision of tests:

* for some reason, I omitted passing the `null-eof` client option for UDP `socat` tests starting from some rebase. Now it's back. Tests would generally terminate due to the `shut-null` option on the server plus the fact that the client would usually include an empty datagram at the end, but this is not reliable
* instead of pinging the address of the default gateway, for ICMP and ICMPv6 echo tests, I'm now pinging the address of the first resolver configured in the container. Rationale: the gateway in the CI environment doesn't answer echo requests.

Ci failures on the lines of:

> :exclamation: Failed to start: Task java.util.concurrent.ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor$ScheduledFutureTask@2a8ed2f[Not completed, task = java.util.concurrent.Executors$RunnableAdapter@7ac34199[Wrapped task = TrustedListenableFutureTask@70f8ba9f[status=PENDING, info=[task=[running=[NOT STARTED YET], com.google.api.gax.rpc.CheckingAttemptCallable@19a0b89f]]]]] rejected from java.util.concurrent.ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor@6207ab6a[Terminated, pool size = 0, active threads = 0, queued tasks = 0, completed tasks = 0]

...no idea what that might be.
That's usually a CI provider issue. Manually restarted.

> instead of pinging the address of the default gateway, for ICMP and ICMPv6 echo tests, I'm now pinging the address of the first resolver configured in the container. Rationale: the gateway in the CI environment doesn't answer echo requests.

Will this work reliably? I am not so sure. The tests are run locally on developers laptops, in podman CI with the google cloud infra and downstream gating testing in fedora and RHEL. It is always a huge pain if this stuff breaks downstream.

> > instead of pinging the address of the default gateway, for ICMP and ICMPv6 echo tests, I'm now pinging the address of the first resolver configured in the container. Rationale: the gateway in the CI environment doesn't answer echo requests.
> 
> Will this work reliably? I am not so sure. The tests are run locally on developers laptops, in podman CI with the google cloud infra and downstream gating testing in fedora and RHEL. It is always a huge pain if this stuff breaks downstream.

I'm not entirely sure either, but it's probably our best bet. I tested it on the CI system, on my laptop, on my server. The only alternative I see is to skip the ICMP tests altogether. I'm not so fond of it because you already found actual issues with them.
